title: The $p$-curvature conjecture and monodromy about simple closed loops
speaker: Ananth Shankar
abstract: The Grothendieck-Katz $p$-curvature conjecture is an analogue of the Hasse Principle for differential equations. It states that a set of arithmetic differential equations on a variety has finite monodromy if its $p$-curvature vanishes modulo $p$, for almost all primes $p$. We prove that if the variety is a generic curve, then every simple closed loop has finite monodromy.

proof_stages.label: Connections and algebraic solutions on curves
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a smooth algebraic curve with a vector bundle and connection, defines rational and algebraic solutions, and formulates the problem of finding an arithmetic criterion for a full set of algebraic solutions.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-zero monodromy interpretation
proof_stages.summary: Over the complex numbers, local holomorphic solutions form a local system, so global analytic solutions correspond to trivial monodromy and algebraic solutions after finite pullback correspond, under regular singularity hypotheses, to finite monodromy.

proof_stages.label: p-curvature and the Grothendieck-Katz reduction
proof_stages.summary: In characteristic p the p-curvature is defined and Cartier's theorem identifies its vanishing with a full set of solutions, leading to the Grothendieck-Katz conjecture and its reduction, via Katz regular-singularity results, to proving finite monodromy from almost-everywhere vanishing p-curvature.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem for generic curves
proof_stages.summary: The central theorem is stated: for a generic punctured curve, vanishing p-curvature for almost all primes implies that every simple closed loop has finite monodromy; the genericity condition is defined and its strength and limitations are discussed.

proof_stages.label: Degeneration of simple closed loops to nodes
proof_stages.summary: Using genericity, the curve is spread out in a family so that any simple closed loop can be pinched to a node on a boundary fiber, reducing the desired finite-monodromy statement to a local analysis near such a nodal degeneration.

proof_stages.label: Rigid-analytic annulus theorem near the node
proof_stages.summary: The formal neighborhood of the node is replaced by a rigid analytic annulus over a q-adic base, and the key rigid theorem asserts extension of the connection matrix to the special fiber and, after finite pullback and base change to Q, existence of a full set of solutions.

proof_stages.label: q-adic normalization using the G_m special fiber
proof_stages.summary: After extension, reduction modulo powers of q identifies the special fiber with G_m; using the known G_m case and an inductive gauge transformation that integrates nonconstant Laurent terms, the connection matrix is made independent of the annulus coordinate modulo all powers of q.

proof_stages.label: p-curvature kills the constant term
proof_stages.summary: For the remaining q-divisible constant connection matrix, vanishing p-curvature gives A^p = A modulo q^n for large p, forcing A to vanish modulo every q^n and hence yielding rigid, then holomorphic, solutions and finite monodromy around the pinched loop.

examples.name: Thrice-punctured projective line
examples.note: The case of P1 minus three points is mentioned in response to a question as a low-complexity generic curve where simple closed loops reduce to local monodromy, illustrating how the theorem recovers Katz's local-monodromy finiteness result.
examples.engagement_reason: The thrice-punctured projective line is only cited as a generic case where simple closed loops are local monodromy, with no calculation or construction carried out on it.

passes.reason: The thrice-punctured projective line is the minimal stable genus-zero punctured curve with no moduli, used only as Katz’s local-monodromy case.
It is the minimal stable/hyperbolic punctured curve, with M_{0,3} a point, used only as a base case for local monodromy.
The thrice-punctured projective line is the minimal zero-moduli stable hyperbolic curve, used only to recover Katz's local-monodromy case.
The thrice-punctured projective line is the minimal stable/hyperbolic punctured sphere, used here as the special case where simple-loop monodromy reduces to local monodromy, not to refute a claim.
the thrice-punctured projective line is the minimal zero-moduli hyperbolic punctured curve, used only as the local-monodromy special case
The thrice-punctured sphere is the minimal stable hyperbolic punctured curve, used only as a special case recovering local monodromy.
rigid minimal stable punctured sphere with interchangeable cusps, used only as the local-monodromy special case
Minimal rigid hyperbolic punctured sphere with all essential simple loops peripheral and S3 symmetry, used as a positive local-monodromy case.
P^1 minus three points is the rigid minimal stable punctured sphere, used only to recover Katz’s local-monodromy case.
It is used as a special case of the theorem, and structurally it is the rigid minimal hyperbolic punctured sphere of type (0,3).
The thrice-punctured sphere is the zero-moduli, minimal-complexity pair of pants with full permutation symmetry, used only as a base case for local monodromy.
the thrice-punctured sphere is the rigid minimal hyperbolic punctured curve, used only as the local-monodromy special case

examples.name: TQFT representations with finite simple-loop monodromy
examples.note: Representations from topological quantum field theory are cited as counterexamples where every simple closed loop has finite, even bounded, order while the total image is infinite, showing why the stated theorem alone cannot imply the full p-curvature conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: The TQFT representations are invoked as known counterexamples with finite simple-loop monodromy but infinite image, without describing or running their construction.

passes.reason: The TQFT representations are invoked to refute the implication that finite simple-loop monodromy forces finite total monodromy.
These systematic TQFT representations show that finite simple-loop monodromy does not imply finite total monodromy image.
These TQFT representation families have finite simple-loop monodromy but infinite image, refuting that simple-loop finiteness alone forces finite monodromy.
These TQFT representations are cited to show that finite order on every simple closed loop need not imply finite total monodromy image.
the TQFT representations are used to show finite simple-loop monodromy need not imply finite total monodromy
These are systematic TQFT representation examples used to show finite simple-loop monodromy need not imply finite total monodromy.
a systematic TQFT family used to refute that finite simple-loop monodromy forces finite total monodromy image
Used to show finite even bounded simple-loop monodromy does not force finite total monodromy image.
These TQFT representations are used to refute the implication that finite simple-loop monodromy forces finite total monodromy.
These TQFT representations are invoked to show that finite monodromy on every simple closed loop need not imply finite total image.

examples.name: Rank-one connection on G_m
examples.note: A Katz calculation for a line bundle on G_m with derivation t d/dt shows concretely how vanishing p-curvature forces a rational exponent and hence an algebraic solution after taking roots of t, foreshadowing the later annulus calculation.
examples.engagement_reason: The rank-one connection on G_m is explicitly computed: the speaker chooses t d/dt, derives the p-curvature condition lambda^p=lambda, concludes lambda is rational, and constructs/checks the algebraic solution t^{-a/b}epsilon.

passes.reason: The connection is a rank-one line-bundle case on the one-dimensional torus, serving as a minimal base-case computation rather than an obstruction.
A line-bundle connection on G_m is the rank-one minimal case used for Katz’s illustrative calculation.
A rank-one connection on the torus G_m is the minimal abelian model used for Katz's illustrative calculation, not to disprove a claim.
A rank-one connection on G_m is the minimal line-bundle case used as a basic illustrative calculation of the p-curvature criterion, not as a counterexample.
the rank-one connection on the one-dimensional torus is a minimal-rank test case for the p-curvature calculation, not a refutation
The example is the minimal-rank line-connection case on the one-dimensional torus, used as an illustrative Katz calculation rather than to refute a claim.
minimal-rank line-bundle connection on G_m used as an illustrative Katz calculation
Minimal-rank scalar connection on the one-dimensional torus, a homogeneous model calculation rather than a refutation.
